enrichment.short_description: A self-hosted, Go-powered comment system that adds Disqus-style commenting to any website without third-party tracking.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Artalk is an open-source, self-hosted comment system built to give website owners a Disqus-like commenting experience without handing visitor data to a third party. The backend is written in Go and ships as a single lightweight binary or Docker image, while the embeddable frontend widget is built in vanilla JavaScript with no framework dependency, keeping the client-side footprint small.
The project's GitHub repository was created in 2018 and remains actively developed, with documentation and community support maintained in both English and Chinese.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Artalk targets bloggers, documentation sites, and developers who run static sites or CMS-driven blogs and want full control over comment data and moderation, rather than outsourcing to a hosted SaaS commenting service.

features.title: Comment moderation & spam interception
features.description: Built-in content detection, rate limiting, and captcha challenges to filter spam.

features.title: Multi-channel notifications
features.description: Email alerts with customizable templates plus additional push notification channels for new comments.

features.title: Markdown & LaTeX support
features.description: Commenters can format replies with Markdown syntax and render math formulas.

features.title: Image uploads with lightbox
features.description: Attach images to comments with configurable storage and a built-in image viewer.

features.title: Multi-site admin dashboard
features.description: Manage comments across multiple websites from a single centralized admin panel.

features.title: Social login
features.description: Visitors can sign in with third-party accounts instead of creating a new login.

features.title: Comment voting, sorting & search
features.description: Readers can upvote comments, sort threads, and search through comment history.

features.title: Page view counter
features.description: Tracks and displays page view statistics alongside comments.

pros: Fully self-hosted, so comment data never leaves your own server
Lightweight Go backend and a small vanilla-JS widget keep resource usage low
Free and open source under the MIT license
Actively maintained with frequent releases and an OpenAPI-documented API
Works with any blog, static site generator, or web app regardless of platform
cons: Requires running and maintaining your own backend service (Go binary or Docker container)
Smaller ecosystem and community than mainstream hosted options like Disqus
Primary documentation and community threads are bilingual (English/Chinese), which can be harder to follow for some users

faqs.question: Is Artalk free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Artalk is free, open-source software licensed under MIT. You self-host it, so the only cost is your own server infrastructure.

faqs.question: What do I need to run Artalk?
faqs.answer: Artalk's backend is a single Go binary or Docker container; the frontend is a small JavaScript snippet you embed on your site.

faqs.question: Can I migrate comments from another system?
faqs.answer: Artalk documents data migration tooling for importing existing comments from other platforms.

faqs.question: Does Artalk support moderation?
faqs.answer: Yes — it includes spam and content detection, captcha, and an admin sidebar for reviewing and managing comments across sites.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted blog comments
use_cases.description: Bloggers using static site generators can embed Artalk's lightweight widget to add commenting without relying on Disqus.

use_cases.title: Multi-site publishers
use_cases.description: Site owners running several blogs or docs sites can manage comments across all of them from one Artalk admin dashboard.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Artalk is a free, self-hosted comment system written in Go with Markdown, moderation, and notification support for any website.

enrichment.short_description: A lightweight, self-hosted forum engine written entirely in x86-64 assembly language for minimal resource use.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: AsmBB is an open-source forum and bulletin-board engine notable for being written entirely in x86-64 assembly language rather than a conventional web framework, using SQLite as its datastore. It is developed and maintained by John Found, and the project's own community board (board.asm32.info) runs on the software itself.
The engine is built with the Fresh IDE toolchain and organizes discussions with a flexible tagging system instead of rigid sub-forum categories.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: AsmBB suits developers and technical hobbyist communities — particularly assembly-language and systems-programming forums — who want a minimal-footprint, fast-loading discussion board and don't mind trading a large plugin ecosystem for raw performance and a tiny codebase.

features.title: Tag-based organization
features.description: Threads are organized with a flexible tagging system rather than rigid sub-forum categories.

features.title: BBCode formatting
features.description: Posts support BBCode markup for text formatting.

features.title: User registration & authentication
features.description: Built-in account system for community members.

features.title: Email integration
features.description: Supports email-based notifications and account features.

features.title: Multi-language interface
features.description: UI available in multiple languages, including Russian and Chinese, alongside English.

features.title: Theme/skin customization
features.description: Multiple visual skins can be applied to a board.

features.title: File attachments
features.description: Users can attach files to forum posts.

features.title: Assembly-based performance
features.description: The engine compiles to native x86-64 code via the Fresh IDE toolchain, minimizing runtime overhead.

pros: Extremely lightweight and fast due to native assembly implementation
Free and open source under the EUPL license
Low resource requirements, suitable for small VPS instances
Simple SQLite-based storage with no heavyweight database server needed
cons: Written in assembly language, making it very difficult for most developers to customize, audit, or contribute to
Small maintainer base, primarily one developer, which is a risk for long-term support
Limited plugin and theme ecosystem compared to mainstream forum software like Discourse or phpBB
Building from source requires the niche Fresh IDE toolchain

faqs.question: What language is AsmBB written in?
faqs.answer: AsmBB is written in x86-64 assembly language, built using the Fresh IDE toolchain.

faqs.question: Is AsmBB free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open source under the European Union Public License (EUPL).

faqs.question: What database does AsmBB use?
faqs.answer: SQLite.

faqs.question: Who maintains AsmBB?
faqs.answer: It's developed and maintained by John Found, with an official demo community running at board.asm32.info.

use_cases.title: Assembly and systems-programming communities
use_cases.description: Technical communities centered on low-level programming can run a forum that reflects the same performance philosophy as their subject matter.

use_cases.title: Resource-constrained self-hosting
use_cases.description: Operators wanting a discussion board that runs comfortably on minimal VPS resources without a heavy database server.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: AsmBB is a free, open-source forum engine written in x86-64 assembly with SQLite storage, tagging, and BBCode support.

enrichment.short_description: A free WordPress plugin that turns any WordPress site into a full-featured social community with profiles, groups, and activity streams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: BuddyPress is a free WordPress plugin, first released in 2008 by Andy Peatling, that adds social-networking features — member profiles, activity streams, groups, and private messaging — on top of any WordPress installation. Stewardship passed from Automattic to the WordPress Foundation in 2010, and the plugin remains under active community development, with releases tested against current WordPress versions.
BuddyPress is modular: administrators can enable only the components they want, and the interface adapts to show just those features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: BuddyPress is aimed at WordPress site owners who want to build a community around their site — universities running alumni or student networks, companies building internal communication tools, or niche communities and membership sites — without switching away from WordPress.

features.title: Extended member profiles
features.description: Customizable profile fields beyond the standard WordPress user profile.

features.title: Activity streams
features.description: A real-time feed of member activity, similar to a social network timeline.

features.title: User groups
features.description: Members can create and join public or private groups for sub-communities.

features.title: Private messaging
features.description: Built-in direct messaging between members.

features.title: Notifications
features.description: In-app and email notifications for social activity.

features.title: bbPress forum integration
features.description: Connects with the bbPress plugin to add discussion forums to groups.

features.title: Modular components
features.description: Individual features such as groups, messaging, and activity can be enabled or disabled independently.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Exposes BuddyPress data through the WordPress REST API for custom integrations and apps.

pros: Free and open source (GPL), with no licensing cost regardless of site size
Deep integration with the existing WordPress ecosystem of themes, plugins, and users
Modular design lets administrators enable only the features they need
Long track record since 2008 with an active core team and large community of extensions
Backed institutionally via the WordPress Foundation and long-term contributors like Automattic
cons: Requires a self-managed WordPress installation and hosting, not a hosted SaaS product
Can add meaningful overhead to a WordPress site as more components and plugins are enabled
Community and social features often require additional theme and plugin work to look polished out of the box
Smaller dedicated plugin marketplace compared to standalone community platforms

faqs.question: Is BuddyPress free?
faqs.answer: Yes, BuddyPress is free, open-source software released under the GPL, available directly from WordPress.org.

faqs.question: Do I need WordPress to use BuddyPress?
faqs.answer: Yes, BuddyPress is a WordPress plugin and requires an existing or new WordPress installation to run.

faqs.question: Does BuddyPress include forums?
faqs.answer: Discussion forums are added through integration with the companion bbPress plugin.

faqs.question: Who maintains BuddyPress?
faqs.answer: It's maintained by a volunteer core team, including long-time leads John James Jacoby, Boone B. Gorges, and Paul Gibbs, under the WordPress Foundation, with contributions from companies like Automattic and WebDevStudios.

use_cases.title: Education and alumni networks
use_cases.description: Universities and schools use BuddyPress to run private social networks for students, staff, or alumni on top of their existing WordPress site.

use_cases.title: Membership and niche communities
use_cases.description: Site owners running interest-based or membership communities can add profiles, groups, and activity feeds without leaving WordPress.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: BuddyPress is a free, open-source WordPress plugin that adds member profiles, groups, activity streams, and messaging to any site.

enrichment.short_description: An open-source commenting and community platform built for news publishers, self-hostable or offered as a managed service by Vox Media.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Coral began in 2014 as "The Coral Project," a collaboration between Mozilla, The New York Times, and The Washington Post funded by the Knight Foundation to build open-source tools for online comments. Mozilla continued the project after 2017, and Vox Media acquired it in 2019, where it's maintained today as an open-source commenting and community platform for news organizations, alongside an optional Vox-Media-hosted managed offering.
Self-hosting Coral requires Node.js, MongoDB, and Redis, with Docker recommended to bundle those dependencies for deployment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Coral is purpose-built for newsrooms and publishers that want moderation-heavy, ad-free, tracker-free commenting on their articles — from small independent outlets self-hosting the open-source code to larger publishers using Vox Media's managed hosting and support.

features.title: Moderation tooling
features.description: Dashboards for reviewing, filtering, and moderating comments, including AI-assisted moderation aids.

features.title: Live Q&A
features.description: A real-time question-and-answer format for reader engagement events.

features.title: Identity & reputation tools
features.description: Commenter verification, muting, and history tracking for moderators.

features.title: GraphQL API
features.description: The platform is built around a GraphQL API for customization and integration into publisher workflows.

features.title: No ads or trackers
features.description: The comment stream carries no advertising or third-party tracking scripts.

features.title: Notifications
features.description: Alerts for commenters on replies and moderation actions.

features.title: Mobile-responsive embed
features.description: The comment stream is designed to work across device sizes.

pros: Open-source (Apache 2.0) and free to self-host with no per-comment or per-user licensing fee
Purpose-built for journalism and news use cases, with moderation features tuned for high-volume public commenting
Backed by an established media company (Vox Media) with an optional managed-hosting path for teams that don't want to run their own infrastructure
No ads or tracking scripts embedded in the comment experience
cons: Self-hosting requires a Node.js/GraphQL stack plus MongoDB and Redis, which adds real operational overhead
Narrower focus than general-purpose community platforms, built specifically around article commenting rather than broader social features
Managed hosting and support pricing from Vox Media isn't publicly listed and requires contacting sales

faqs.question: Is Coral free?
faqs.answer: The core software is free and open source (Apache 2.0); Vox Media also offers a paid managed-hosting option for publishers who don't want to self-host.

faqs.question: What do I need to self-host Coral?
faqs.answer: A server running Node.js, MongoDB, and Redis. The project recommends deploying via Docker to bundle these dependencies.

faqs.question: Who built Coral?
faqs.answer: It originated as The Coral Project (Mozilla, The New York Times, The Washington Post, funded by the Knight Foundation) in 2014, and has been maintained by Vox Media since 2019.

faqs.question: Does Coral support live events?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes a live Q&A tool for real-time reader engagement.

use_cases.title: Newsroom comment sections
use_cases.description: Publishers replace generic comment widgets with a moderation-first, ad-free comment stream tailored to news articles.

use_cases.title: Reader Q&A events
use_cases.description: Outlets running live coverage or AMA-style events use Coral's live Q&A tool to manage reader questions in real time.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Coral is an open-source, self-hostable commenting platform built for news publishers, with moderation tools and an optional managed hosting service.

enrichment.short_description: A privacy-focused, decentralized social network made of independently run, federated pods rather than one central server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: diaspora* launched in 2010 after a Kickstarter campaign by four NYU students — Dan Grippi, Maxwell Salzberg, Raphael Sofaer, and Ilya Zhitomirskiy — with the goal of building a decentralized alternative to centralized social networks. The network runs as a collection of independently operated 'pods' that federate with one another over diaspora*'s own protocol, so no single company controls the whole network.
The project is now stewarded by the nonprofit Diaspora Foundation and continues to receive updates, though at a slower pace than newer fediverse projects.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: diaspora* is aimed at privacy-conscious users and communities who want to run or join a social network outside of a corporate platform's data-collection model, and at self-hosters comfortable operating a Ruby on Rails application.

features.title: Aspect-based sharing
features.description: Posts can be shared with specific defined groups of contacts ("aspects") rather than an all-or-nothing audience.

features.title: Federation across pods
features.description: Independently hosted pods interconnect so users on different pods can still follow and interact with each other.

features.title: No ads or tracking by design
features.description: The platform's stated philosophy excludes advertising and third-party tracking.

features.title: Hashtag following
features.description: Users can follow hashtags across the federated network, not just people.

features.title: Polls
features.description: Built-in support for posting polls.

features.title: Photo & video sharing
features.description: Native media attachments in posts.

features.title: Public API
features.description: diaspora* exposes an API that third-party clients and integrations can build on.

pros: Fully decentralized, with no single company or server controlling the network
Free, open source (AGPL-3.0), and can be joined via an existing public pod without running your own server
Granular "aspects" sharing gives fine control over who sees each post
Long-running project since 2010 with an established, if small, community
cons: Much smaller active user base than mainstream or newer federated platforms like Mastodon
Development pace is slow, and its federation protocol predates and differs from ActivityPub, limiting interoperability with the wider fediverse
Self-hosting a pod requires operating a Ruby on Rails stack, which is more involved than simpler tools
Choosing and trusting a pod operator matters if not self-hosting, since pod admins can see hosted user data

faqs.question: Do I need to run my own server to use diaspora*?
faqs.answer: No — you can join any existing public pod, or self-host your own pod if you want full control.

faqs.question: Is diaspora* free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: How is diaspora* different from Mastodon?
faqs.answer: diaspora* uses its own federation protocol and organizes sharing around "aspects" (custom audience groups) rather than the ActivityPub-based follow model most fediverse apps use.

faqs.question: Who maintains diaspora*?
faqs.answer: The nonprofit Diaspora Foundation, part of the Free Software Support Network.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused social networking
use_cases.description: Individuals wanting to leave centralized social platforms can join a community-run pod or host their own.

use_cases.title: Community-run networks
use_cases.description: Groups can operate their own diaspora* pod to host a self-governed social space for their members.
use_cases.audience: Communities and organizations wanting an independent social network

enrichment.seo_meta_description: diaspora* is a free, open-source decentralized social network made of federated pods, giving users control over their own data and audience.

enrichment.short_description: An open-source PHP framework for building custom social networking and community platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Elgg is an open-source social networking engine, originally created in 2004 by Ben Werdmuller and Dave Tosh, that developers use as a framework or toolkit to build custom community platforms rather than a turnkey social network. Governance moved to the nonprofit Elgg Foundation in 2010, and the project has continued through major PHP-based version releases since, with version 7 as its current line.
Rather than shipping a fixed set of social features, Elgg provides a plugin, entity, and event-hook architecture that developers extend to fit a specific community's needs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Elgg is aimed at developers and organizations — universities, NGOs, government agencies, and enterprises — that need to build a bespoke social or community platform, such as intranets, communities of practice, or learning networks, and want a modular, extensible PHP foundation rather than starting from scratch.

features.title: Plugin/extension architecture
features.description: A modular plugin system with a large library of community-built plugins.

features.title: Composer-based packaging
features.description: Dependencies and plugins are managed through Composer.

features.title: Entity system
features.description: A flexible data model for defining custom content types.

features.title: Access control & groups
features.description: Fine-grained, per-item access permissions and built-in user groups.

features.title: Event & hook system
features.description: Developers can tap into Elgg's event and view hooks to customize behavior without modifying core.

features.title: File storage & notifications
features.description: Built-in file handling and a notification system.

features.title: Internationalization
features.description: Core support for translating the platform into multiple languages.

features.title: Security protections
features.description: Built-in CSRF and XSS protections.

pros: Free and open source, dual-licensed under GPLv2 and MIT
Highly extensible plugin and event architecture suited to building bespoke community platforms
Long track record since 2004 with a stable nonprofit steward, the Elgg Foundation
Strong access-control model for building platforms with complex privacy requirements
cons: It's a framework, not an out-of-the-box social network — real use requires development effort to configure plugins and design an experience
PHP/MySQL stack and smaller community than newer JavaScript-based community platforms
Has a learning curve around its plugin, event, and hook system for new developers

faqs.question: Is Elgg free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Elgg is free and open source, dual-licensed under GPLv2 and MIT.

faqs.question: Do I need to know how to code to use Elgg?
faqs.answer: Elgg is a development framework — building a full community site typically requires PHP development or configuring existing community plugins.

faqs.question: What does Elgg run on?
faqs.answer: Elgg is written in PHP and uses a MySQL-compatible database, running on a standard Apache or Nginx web server.

faqs.question: Who maintains Elgg?
faqs.answer: The nonprofit Elgg Foundation, which took over stewardship in 2010.

use_cases.title: Organizational intranets
use_cases.description: Companies and NGOs build internal community and knowledge-sharing platforms on top of Elgg's plugin system.

use_cases.title: Educational communities of practice
use_cases.description: Universities and training organizations use Elgg to build learning-focused social platforms.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Elgg is a free, open-source PHP framework for building custom social networking and community platforms, maintained by the Elgg Foundation.

enrichment.short_description: A modern, cross-platform bulletin board system server that recreates the classic dial-up terminal community experience over Telnet and SSH.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ENiGMA½ (ENiGMA 1/2) is an open-source BBS server, first published on GitHub in 2015 by developer NuSkooler, that lets operators run a modern, multi-node terminal community reachable over Telnet, SSH, and WebSockets. It brings the retro dial-up bulletin board format — message boards, file areas, door games — to contemporary server infrastructure built on Node.js.
The project remains actively maintained, with a large commit history and ongoing releases, and is highly configurable through JavaScript-based menus, themes, and "mods."

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: ENiGMA½ is built for BBS hobbyists, sysops, and retrocomputing/ANSI-art communities who want to run a multi-node terminal bulletin board rather than a web-based forum.

features.title: Multi-protocol access
features.description: Supports Telnet, SSH, and WebSocket connections for terminal clients.

features.title: ANSI/ASCII art support
features.description: Renders ANSI, CP437, and UTF-8 art, including SAUCE metadata.

features.title: FidoNet/BinkP networking
features.description: Can participate in FidoNet-style inter-BBS message networks over BinkP.

features.title: Door game support
features.description: Runs classic DOS door games via built-in x86 emulation, plus Z-Machine interactive fiction.

features.title: File areas
features.description: A searchable file base with HTTP download links for uploaded files.

features.title: Customizable menus & mods
features.description: JavaScript-based menu system and "mods" for building custom BBS experiences and themes.

features.title: Security
features.description: PBKDF2 password hashing with two-factor authentication support.

features.title: Built-in extra servers
features.description: Includes its own HTTPS web server plus Gopher and NNTP servers.

pros: Free and open source under the BSD-2-Clause license
Actively maintained, with a large commit history and recent releases
Rich protocol support spanning Telnet, SSH, WebSocket, FidoNet, Gopher, and NNTP, beyond a typical single-purpose BBS
Highly customizable via a JavaScript-based menu and mod system
cons: Aimed at a niche, retro-computing audience rather than mainstream community-building use cases
Requires comfort running and administering a Node.js server
No polished graphical installer — setup is config-file driven (HJSON)

faqs.question: Is ENiGMA½ free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the BSD-2-Clause license.

faqs.question: What do I need to run it?
faqs.answer: A server running Node.js; data is stored in SQLite, and configuration is done through HJSON files.

faqs.question: Can users connect the same way they did to classic BBSes?
faqs.answer: Yes — ENiGMA½ supports Telnet and SSH connections in addition to modern WebSocket access.

faqs.question: Does it support door games?
faqs.answer: Yes, including native DOS door game emulation and Z-Machine interactive fiction support.

use_cases.title: Retro BBS communities
use_cases.description: Hobbyists stand up a classic-style multi-node bulletin board reachable over Telnet/SSH for message boards and file sharing.

use_cases.title: FidoNet-connected boards
use_cases.description: Operators link their board into the wider FidoNet message network using built-in BinkP support.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: ENiGMA half BBS is a free, open-source BBS server supporting Telnet, SSH, and WebSocket access, ANSI art, door games, and FidoNet networking.

enrichment.short_description: A lightweight, self-hosted ActivityPub server that provides Mastodon-API-compatible federated social networking with a small resource footprint.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: GoToSocial is an open-source ActivityPub server, started as a solo project around 2021 by a small team known as Super Serious Business, designed to be a lightweight alternative to Mastodon for running a single-user or small-group fediverse server. It's written in Go and is explicitly built around the idea of running "many small, weird, specialist servers" rather than large centralized instances.
The project has been in beta since late 2024 and continues active development toward a stable release, with its canonical codebase hosted on Codeberg.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: GoToSocial suits individuals and small communities who want to self-host a fediverse presence with minimal server resources, without the algorithmic-feed and large-instance dynamics of bigger platforms.

features.title: Chronological timelines
features.description: No algorithmic ranking; posts appear in time order.

features.title: Granular visibility controls
features.description: Public, unlisted, followers-only, and direct-message-style post visibility.

features.title: Reply-interaction policies
features.description: Fine-grained control over who can reply to a given post.

features.title: Mastodon-API compatibility
features.description: Existing Mastodon-compatible mobile and desktop apps can connect to a GoToSocial account.

features.title: Markdown post formatting
features.description: Posts can be composed using Markdown.

features.title: Theming
features.description: Custom CSS and profile theming options.

features.title: Built-in HTTPS
features.description: Automatic HTTPS/Let's Encrypt certificate handling.

features.title: OIDC integration
features.description: Supports OpenID Connect for external authentication.

pros: Free and open source under the AGPLv3 license
Very low resource requirements, roughly 250-350MB of RAM, making it cheap to self-host on small VPS instances
Compatible with existing Mastodon apps via its Mastodon-API-compatible REST API
Actively developed with frequent releases and a small, focused core team
cons: Still in beta as of its current release cycle, not yet a "1.0" stable release
Smaller feature set than Mastodon by design, such as no nested boosting and limited discovery tools
No built-in algorithmic discovery, which reflects the project's philosophy but may not suit everyone

faqs.question: Is GoToSocial free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the AGPLv3 license.

faqs.question: Can I use Mastodon apps with GoToSocial?
faqs.answer: Yes — GoToSocial implements a Mastodon-API-compatible REST API, so most existing Mastodon apps work with it.

faqs.question: Is GoToSocial stable and production-ready?
faqs.answer: It's in active beta; the project has not yet declared a 1.0 stable release.

faqs.question: What database does GoToSocial use?
faqs.answer: SQLite by default, with optional PostgreSQL support.

use_cases.title: Personal fediverse server
use_cases.description: An individual runs their own single-user ActivityPub server with a Mastodon-compatible app instead of joining a large instance.
use_cases.audience: Individuals wanting an independent fediverse presence

use_cases.title: Small-group or community instances
use_cases.description: Small communities run a low-resource federated server for their members without operating a full Mastodon deployment.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: GoToSocial is a free, open-source, lightweight ActivityPub server with a Mastodon-compatible API, built for small fediverse instances.

enrichment.short_description: A self-hosted bridge that adds automated ActivityPub/Fediverse federation to any static blog or website.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Hatsu is an open-source tool, with its GitHub repository created in mid-2023, that lets owners of static sites and blogs gain Fediverse followers and replies without building ActivityPub federation into their site themselves. It reads a site's existing RSS, Atom, or JSON feed as its data source and handles the federation protocol work — accepting follows, pushing new posts to followers, and feeding Fediverse replies back to the site — as a self-hosted, Docker-deployable service.
The project's own site describes Hatsu as still a work in progress, and it is explicitly scoped to ActivityPub rather than other decentralized-social protocols like Nostr or Bluesky's AT Protocol.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Hatsu is for static-site and blog owners on any site generator or static host who want their content to reach Mastodon and other ActivityPub-based Fediverse users without adopting a full dynamic CMS or federation-capable platform.

features.title: Feed-based federation
features.description: Consumes a site's RSS, Atom, or JSON feed to detect new posts and federate them automatically.

features.title: Follow handling
features.description: Accepts and manages Fediverse follow requests on behalf of the static site.

features.title: Reply backfeed
features.description: Captures replies made from the Fediverse and surfaces them back to the site.

features.title: Fediverse discovery redirects
features.description: Resolves Fediverse handle and URL lookups to the correct Hatsu-hosted resource.

features.title: Static-site agnostic
features.description: Works with any static site generator or static hosting provider, since it only needs a feed URL.

features.title: Docker deployment
features.description: Ships as a self-hosted, Docker-deployable service.

features.title: ActivityPub-focused
features.description: Implements ActivityPub federation specifically; it does not bridge to Nostr or AT Protocol/Bluesky.

pros: Free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license
Requires no changes to an existing static site beyond having a syndication feed
Written in Rust and designed to be lightweight to self-host
Actively maintained with recent commits
cons: Explicitly labeled a work in progress by its own maintainers, so expect rough edges
Supports ActivityPub only, not other decentralized-social protocols
Smaller community and ecosystem than more established fediverse tools like GoToSocial or Mastodon
Requires self-hosting know-how, including Docker and DNS/HTTPS setup, to federate correctly

faqs.question: Is Hatsu free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: Do I need to change my static site to use Hatsu?
faqs.answer: No — Hatsu only needs your site's existing RSS, Atom, or JSON feed to detect and federate new posts.

faqs.question: What protocol does Hatsu support?
faqs.answer: ActivityPub, the protocol used by Mastodon and most of the Fediverse; it does not support Nostr or Bluesky's AT Protocol.

faqs.question: Is Hatsu production-ready?
faqs.answer: The project's own documentation describes it as still a work in progress, so expect ongoing changes.

use_cases.title: Static blog federation
use_cases.description: Bloggers on Hugo, Jekyll, Eleventy, or similar static generators can gain Fediverse followers and replies by pointing Hatsu at their feed.

use_cases.title: Fediverse reach without a CMS switch
use_cases.description: Sites that don't want to move to a dynamic, federation-native CMS can still reach Mastodon and ActivityPub users.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Hatsu is a free, open-source, self-hosted bridge that adds ActivityPub/Fediverse federation to any static blog using its existing feed.

enrichment.short_description: A decentralized social networking and publishing platform with nomadic identity that lets accounts move or clone across independently run servers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Hubzilla traces its lineage to Mike Macgirvin's 2010 "Mistpark"/Friendika work, which was forked into "Red"/"RedMatrix" in 2012 and renamed Hubzilla in 2015. It combines social networking, blogging, wikis, and cloud file storage into one federated platform, built around a distinctive "nomadic identity" system that lets a user's account be cloned or moved between independently run Hubzilla servers, called hubs, without losing their connections.
Development has been community-led since 2015, with the codebase hosted on Framagit and ongoing releases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Hubzilla suits communities and organizations that want an all-in-one federated platform combining a social feed, file storage, wikis, and publishing, with strong, granular privacy controls, rather than a single-purpose microblogging server.

features.title: Nomadic identity
features.description: User identities can be cloned or relocated across different Hubzilla servers while retaining connections and content.

features.title: Fine-grained permissions
features.description: Per-post, per-file, or per-group access control rather than simple public/private toggles.

features.title: Federated single sign-on
features.description: Authentication that carries across federated hubs.

features.title: Built-in blogging & wikis
features.description: Native long-form publishing and collaborative wiki pages alongside social posts.

features.title: Cloud file storage
features.description: Permission-based file storage and sharing built into the platform.

features.title: Discussion groups
features.description: Built-in group discussion spaces.

features.title: Multi-protocol federation
features.description: Interoperates with ActivityPub, Diaspora, and its own Zot protocol, reaching users on Mastodon, Misskey, PeerTube, and other federated platforms.

features.title: Content filtering
features.description: Tools for filtering and blocking unwanted content or contacts.

pros: Free and open source under the MIT license
Broader feature set than single-purpose fediverse tools, combining social, storage, wikis, and blogging in one platform
Nomadic identity is a distinctive feature that avoids full lock-in to any one server operator
Interoperates with multiple federation protocols, Zot, ActivityPub, and Diaspora, reaching a wide range of fediverse platforms
cons: Requires PHP/MySQL-compatible hosting, a steeper setup than some newer Go-based fediverse tools
Smaller mindshare and community than Mastodon or other more mainstream fediverse platforms
The breadth of features spanning social, storage, wikis, and blogging can mean a steeper learning curve for new users and admins
Community-led development pace can be slower than commercially backed alternatives

faqs.question: Is Hubzilla free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: What is nomadic identity?
faqs.answer: It's Hubzilla's system for letting a user's account, connections, and content be cloned or moved to a different Hubzilla server without starting over.

faqs.question: What do I need to self-host Hubzilla?
faqs.answer: A PHP environment with a MySQL-compatible database, such as MariaDB, and a standard web server like Apache.

faqs.question: Does Hubzilla work with Mastodon?
faqs.answer: Yes — Hubzilla supports ActivityPub federation alongside its own Zot protocol, so it can interact with Mastodon, Misskey, PeerTube, and other ActivityPub-based platforms.

use_cases.title: All-in-one community hub
use_cases.description: Organizations run a single federated platform that combines social feed, file storage, wikis, and blogging for their members.

use_cases.title: Portable social identity
use_cases.description: Users who want to avoid being locked into one server operator can use nomadic identity to move their account between hubs.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Hubzilla is a free, open-source federated platform combining social networking, blogging, wikis, and cloud storage with nomadic identity.
